meta.title: UK Politics Fact-Check League
meta.description: A daily fact-check of the most-discussed claim in UK politics, scored on a 0-100 truth meter, with a running league table of everyone featured.
meta.note: Every figure below is anchored to a named primary or established secondary source (linked). Where a claim's accuracy depends on the time period or a definition, that is stated explicitly. Verdicts should be re-confirmed against the linked sources before publication.

entries.topic: Is Greater Manchester the UK's fastest-growing city region, growing at twice the national rate?
entries.claim: We are the UK's fastest growing city region. We're proud of that — Greater Manchester has grown at twice the rate of the rest of the country.
entries.speaker: Andy Burnham
entries.role: Mayor of Greater Manchester; newly elected MP for Makerfield and prospective Labour leadership challenger
entries.discussionNote: Andy Burnham's Makerfield by-election win (declared 19 June) and his march towards a leadership challenge to Keir Starmer made his Greater Manchester record the central battleground of the 20-22 June news cycle, with Starmer vowing to 'run' and ~110 Labour MPs reportedly pressing him to go. As international and UK outlets dissected the platform Burnham would run for PM on, his economic boast — that Greater Manchester is 'the UK's fastest growing city region', growing at roughly twice the national rate — moved to the front of the argument: a CNN profile (20 June) and a Fortune 'King of the North' feature (21 June) led on the Manchester boom, while Bloomberg's 'Manchester is Booming' / 'and the areas it left behind' pieces and an Oxford Economics 'Manchesterism and the realities of growth' briefing scrutinised it. With the 3.1%-vs-1.5% growth figure freshly anchored in a January 2026 IFS/GMCA paper and ONS regional data — and just questioned by analysts as a possible statistical artefact — it was the cleanest, most-discussed checkable number attached to Burnham's prime-ministerial pitch in the 24-48 hour window.
entries.explanation: On the official statistics Burnham cites, the headline claim is correct — Greater Manchester really has been the UK's fastest-growing city region, growing at roughly twice the national rate — but a substantial part of that outperformance rests on ONS data that independent analysts have flagged as probably overstated, and the headline GVA-growth figure masks a much weaker picture for living standards, which is why it lands in Mostly True rather than True. On the core number: a January 2026 paper produced for the Greater Manchester Combined Authority (presented at an IFS event) found that, using ONS regional gross value added (GVA) data, Greater Manchester's economy grew about 3.1% a year on average between 2015 and 2023 — roughly double the UK's ~1.5% — making it the fastest-growing of the UK's major city regions, with GVA topping £100bn in 2023. Centre for Cities separately identifies GM as a national leader on productivity growth (the biggest productivity increase of any UK region/mayoralty over two decades, ~31%), and Oxford Economics calls Manchester 'the star performer of the UK economy since 2008', so the 'fastest-growing' and 'twice the rate' framing is well-supported by multiple independent bodies. Two important caveats pull it below True. First, data reliability: much of the post-2019 surge is questioned — the Economics Observatory shows that ONS figures put GM productivity growth jumping from ~0.5% a year (2008-19) to ~3.3% after 2019, but a large slice came from an implausible spike in 'legal and accounting' output near Altrincham (one area recording ~21,000 sector jobs against a typical ~4,000) plus apparent undercounting of self-employment; adjusting for these cuts measured 2019-23 productivity growth from ~14% to roughly 9%, still above the UK but far less of a 'miracle'. Second, distribution and living standards: the growth is concentrated in Manchester city centre, with outer towns such as Wigan, Oldham and Rochdale below the national average; GM's GVA per head and wages remain below the UK average, unemployment is around 5% (above the national rate), and Oxford Economics warns weak income growth and high housing costs are a real challenge. So Burnham's boast is accurate on the official aggregate-GVA measure he uses and corroborated by several independent sources, but it presents a contested, possibly inflated statistic as settled fact and lets a city-centre boom stand in for the whole region.
evidence: Claim/context (19-22 June 2026): as Burnham moved towards a leadership challenge after his Makerfield win, profiles of his mayoral record (CNN, 20 June; Fortune 'King of the North', 21 June; Bloomberg) foregrounded the Manchester boom; Burnham has repeatedly said 'We are the UK's fastest growing city region' (e.g. at the Mipim property conference) and that GM has grown at 'twice the rate of the rest of the country'.

IFS/GMCA paper (January 2026, using ONS regional GVA): Greater Manchester was the fastest-growing part of the UK between 2015 and 2023, growing about 3.1% a year on average versus ~1.5% for the UK as a whole — roughly double the national rate — with the city-region economy topping £100bn in GVA in 2023.

Centre for Cities / productivity: identifies Greater Manchester as a national leader on productivity growth, with the biggest productivity increase of any UK region or mayoralty over the past two decades (~31%), beginning to narrow the gap with London.

Data-reliability caveat (Economics Observatory, 'Have the UK's northern cities really experienced a productivity miracle?'): ONS data show GM productivity growth jumping from ~0.5% a year (2008-19) to ~3.3% post-2019, but a third of output growth came from 'legal and accounting' services (one area near Altrincham recording ~21,000 such jobs against a typical ~4,000 — likely a data error) and self-employment appears undercounted; correcting for both cuts 2019-23 productivity growth from ~14% to about 9%, 'still above UK growth of 3%' but far more modest.

Distribution caveat (Oxford Economics 'Manchesterism and the realities of growth'; Bloomberg): growth is concentrated in Manchester city centre, with outer towns (Wigan, Oldham, Rochdale) below the national average; Oxford Economics warns 'weak income growth and high housing costs pose a significant challenge' and that the centre is 'too weak and too small' to spread benefits as London's does.

Living-standards caveat (ONS): GM's GVA per head and average wages remain below the UK average and unemployment is around 5% (above the national rate), so the aggregate-GVA 'fastest-growing' headline does not translate into above-average prosperity per person.
entries.confidence: High on the headline finding, medium on the precise magnitude. The 3.1%-vs-1.5% (2015-2023) figures, the 'fastest-growing city region' status, the £100bn 2023 GVA and the productivity leadership are corroborated across the January 2026 IFS/GMCA paper (built on ONS regional GVA), Centre for Cities and Oxford Economics. The data-reliability problems (legal/accounting anomaly, self-employment undercount, the ~14%-to-~9% adjustment) come from the Economics Observatory, and the distribution/per-head caveats from Oxford Economics, Bloomberg and ONS. The main soft spots are that Burnham's exact 19-22 June wording is taken from his consistent, repeatedly-reported 'fastest growing city region / twice the rate' framing (e.g. Mipim) rather than a single verbatim by-election-week transcript, and that the headline GVA growth rate is genuinely contested in magnitude even though GM's relative outperformance is not.

entries.topic: Has rough sleeping fallen in Greater Manchester under Andy Burnham?
entries.claim: When I was elected in 2017, I vowed to end rough sleeping in Greater Manchester — and since then rough sleeping here has fallen by 42%, bucking the national trend.
entries.speaker: Andy Burnham
entries.role: Mayor of Greater Manchester; newly elected MP for Makerfield and prospective Labour leadership challenger
entries.discussionNote: With Andy Burnham's Makerfield by-election win (18 June) propelling him to the brink of a leadership challenge to Keir Starmer, the 20-21 June news cycle pivoted to scrutiny of his actual record as Greater Manchester mayor — the platform on which he would run for PM. A widely shared CNN profile ('Andy Burnham: the charismatic mayor almost certain to challenge for Britain's premiership', 20 June) and a Big Issue 'Manchesterism' feature (19 June) both put his flagship homelessness record front and centre, while critical outlets ran 'Burnham promised to solve Manchester homelessness — now it has doubled' counter-pieces. His signature boast — that rough sleeping in Greater Manchester has fallen sharply (commonly cited as ~42% since 2017, or 'more than 57%') against a rising national trend — was the cleanest checkable number attached to his record, and one anchored in official MHCLG/GMCA single-night snapshot statistics, making it the freshest verifiable factual claim of the 24-48 hour window.
entries.explanation: Measured against the official baseline he uses, Burnham's headline figure is accurate, but the 'since 2017' framing hides a sharp recent reversal that the claim needs flagged. On the core number: the MHCLG annual single-night rough sleeping snapshot recorded 268 people sleeping rough in Greater Manchester in autumn 2017 (the year Burnham became mayor) and 154 in autumn 2024 — a fall of about 42%. Over the same period national rough sleeping did NOT fall: England's autumn 2024 snapshot (4,667) was roughly level with the 2017 peak (4,751, about 2% lower) but had risen 20% in 2024 alone and is up 164% since 2010, so Greater Manchester genuinely bucked the national trend over the period — the comparison Burnham draws is fair. (The 'more than 57%' variant some reports cite reflects a fall measured to an earlier, lower year rather than the latest snapshot.) The important missing context is the trajectory: GM rough sleeping fell steeply to a low of just 89 in 2021 — at which point the fall from 2017 was about 67% — but has since RISEN four years in a row, to 154 in 2024 and an estimated 197 in 2025. Measured to the latest 2025 figure, the fall from 268 is closer to 26%, not 42%, and the monthly (rather than single-night) count is far higher — about 335 people across December 2024. So Burnham's 42% is a real, correctly-sourced number against the 2017 baseline and the national-trend comparison holds, but quoting it without noting that rough sleeping has been climbing again since 2021 presents a four-year deterioration as continued progress, which is why it sits in Mostly True rather than True. His Housing First record is a genuine achievement (450+ people housed since 2019 with an ~88% tenancy-sustainment rate, in line with international norms), and he fairly attributes recent rises partly to national policy (frozen housing benefit, asylum-accommodation evictions).
evidence: Claim/context (19-21 June 2026): as Burnham moved towards a leadership challenge after his Makerfield win, profiles of his mayoral record (CNN, 20 June; Big Issue 'Manchesterism', 19 June) foregrounded his homelessness pledge; he has repeatedly said that since his 2017 election rough sleeping in Greater Manchester has fallen (~42%, or 'more than 57%' in some framings) while bucking the national trend.

MHCLG single-night snapshot (official National Statistics): Greater Manchester recorded 268 people sleeping rough in autumn 2017 and 154 in autumn 2024 — a fall of about 42%; GMCA notes this came as the England average rose, so GM bucked the national trend.

England comparison (Rough sleeping snapshot, autumn 2024): 4,667 people on a single night — about 2% below the 2017 peak of 4,751, but up 20% on 2023 and 164% higher than 2010; national rough sleeping rose for the third year running, confirming GM's relative outperformance over the period.

Recent reversal (caveat): GM rough sleeping fell to a low of 89 in 2021 (a ~67% drop from 2017) but has since risen four consecutive years — to 154 in 2024 and an estimated 197 in 2025 (Manchester Mill, 30 May 2026), so measured to 2025 the fall from 268 is closer to 26%, and the trend is now upward, not downward.

Definition caveat: the 42% figure is the single-night snapshot; the broader monthly count is much larger — around 335 people across December 2024 — so '42% fall' captures the snapshot measure, not the total number of people who experience rough sleeping over time.

Housing First / attribution: since GM's Housing First pilot launched in 2019 more than 450 people have been housed with an ~88% tenancy-sustainment rate (in line with international norms per the Social Market Foundation); Burnham attributes recent rises partly to national policy — frozen housing benefit and Home Office asylum-accommodation evictions (ITV News, Dec 2025).
entries.confidence: High — the 268 (2017) and 154 (2024) single-night figures, the ~42% fall, and the bucking of the national trend come directly from MHCLG/GMCA official snapshot statistics, and the England autumn-2024 totals (4,667; ~2% below the 2017 peak; +20% on 2023; +164% since 2010) come from the GOV.UK rough sleeping snapshot. The four-year rise since the 2021 low of 89 to ~197 in 2025 is corroborated by the Manchester Mill's reporting on GMCA data. The main soft spots are that Burnham's exact 20-21 June wording is paraphrased from his consistent, repeatedly-reported framing rather than a single verbatim transcript, and that the 2025 snapshot figure is reported but not yet formally announced by GMCA — but the qualitative findings (a real ~42% fall from the 2017 baseline, a genuine divergence from the national trend, and a four-year upward reversal since 2021) are robust.

entries.topic: Is there a case for a 50p top rate of income tax to fund public services?
entries.claim: There is definitely a case to bring back the 50p top rate of income tax, so the wealthiest pay their fair share and we can invest in the places Westminster has forgotten.
entries.speaker: Andy Burnham
entries.role: Mayor of Greater Manchester; MP-elect for Makerfield and prospective Labour leadership challenger
entries.discussionNote: Andy Burnham's Makerfield by-election win in the early hours of 19 June 2026 — taking 54.8% of the vote and a majority of around 9,200 over Reform UK — was the single biggest UK political story of the 24-48 hour window, paving the way for a leadership challenge to Keir Starmer, who vowed he 'will not walk away' and 'will run'. The economic dividing line was the most-scrutinised substance of the story: Burnham's case for restoring the 50p top rate of income tax (and a council-tax revaluation to 'tax wealth, not work') drove the markets angle — Bloomberg and CNBC reporting jittery gilt yields and 'wealth tax fears' tied to his candidacy — and prompted Starmer to compare Burnham's plans to Liz Truss's mini-budget 'disaster'. With the 50p rate being the cleanest checkable number in the row and one repeatedly analysed by HMRC, the OBR, the IFS and the House of Commons Library, it was the freshest verifiable factual claim of the window.
entries.explanation: Burnham's claim is carefully hedged — he says there is 'a case' for a 50p rate, not that it would raise a specific sum — and on that narrow framing he is defensible, because the revenue question is genuinely contested rather than settled against him. But the strong implication that a 50p rate is a reliable way to make the wealthy 'pay their fair share' and fund public services overstates what the evidence supports, which keeps the claim in Half True / Needs Context. The mechanics: the UK's additional rate currently applies at 45p on income above £125,140; Burnham wants to restore the 50p rate that operated from April 2010 to April 2013. The central evidence base is HMRC's 2012 analysis of that period, which all the major bodies rely on. HMRC's central estimate was that cutting the rate from 50p back to 45p had a 'static' cost of about £3.0-3.4 billion a year, but once behavioural responses are included the net cost was only about £100 million — implying the 50p rate itself raised very little. That hinges on a taxable income elasticity (TIE) of 0.45, broadly consistent with IFS estimates from the 1980s. The IFS conclusion is blunt: a 50p rate would 'raise little revenue and make, at best, a marginal contribution' to the public finances, and the then-OBR chair described the UK as 'strolling across the summit of the Laffer curve' — i.e. close to the revenue-maximising rate, with limited room to raise more. The OBR separately cut its estimate of the 50p rate's underlying yield from about £2.6 billion to about £0.6 billion as behavioural responses proved larger than first assumed. Crucially, though, the uncertainty is wide and runs in both directions: HMRC and the IFS note that if the true elasticity were lower (around 0.35) the rate could raise meaningful sums, and if higher (0.55) it could lose money — so '50p raises nothing' is not a settled fact either, and a genuine 'case' exists. So Burnham is right that there is a debate to be had, but he glosses over the central-estimate finding that the rate is likely to raise little, which is the context the claim needs.
evidence: Claim/context (18-19 June 2026): after winning the Makerfield by-election (54.8% of the vote; majority ~9,200 over Reform UK), Burnham renewed his case for restoring the 50p top rate of income tax and a council-tax revaluation to 'tax wealth, not work'; he has framed the UK as having 'overtaxed labour and undertaxed wealth'. Starmer responded by likening Burnham's plans to Liz Truss's mini-budget.
Current law: the additional rate is 45p on income above £125,140; the 50p rate Burnham wants to restore was in force from 6 April 2010 to 5 April 2013 before being cut to 45p.

HMRC (2012 analysis, the basis for all subsequent assessments): the 'static' cost of cutting the rate from 50p to 45p was about £3.0-3.4 billion a year, but the central estimate once behavioural responses are included was a net cost of only about £100 million — implying the 50p rate raised very little — using a taxable income elasticity (TIE) of 0.45.

IFS: concludes a 50p rate would 'raise little revenue and make, at best, a marginal contribution' to deficit reduction, and notes the then-OBR chair's verdict that the UK was 'strolling across the summit of the Laffer curve' (close to the revenue-maximising rate).

OBR: reduced its estimate of the underlying yield from the 50p rate (for 2012-13) from about £2.6 billion to about £0.6 billion, because 'the behavioural response to the increase in the additional rate had been greater than originally assumed', while stressing the figure is very hard to estimate due to forestalling (high earners shifted at least £16bn of income forward to be taxed at 40p, a one-off cost of around £1bn).

Two-sided uncertainty: HMRC and the IFS note that if the true elasticity were nearer 0.35 the rate could raise roughly £600-700 million, while at 0.55 it could lose money — so the revenue is genuinely uncertain and a 'case' for the policy can be made, even though the central estimate points to little net gain.
entries.confidence: High — the £100 million net-cost central estimate, the ~£3.0-3.4 billion static cost, the 0.45 elasticity and the IFS 'raise little revenue' / 'summit of the Laffer curve' conclusions are corroborated across HMRC's 2012 analysis, the IFS, the OBR and the House of Commons Library briefing SN00249, and the OBR's downward revision from £2.6bn to £0.6bn is on the record. The main soft spots are that Burnham's exact 18-19 June wording is paraphrased from his renewed 'definitely a case' framing reported around the by-election rather than a single verbatim transcript, and that the underlying HMRC/OBR estimates date from the 2010-13 episode, so the precise revenue under today's tax base and thresholds would differ — but the qualitative finding (highly uncertain, central estimate small) is robust.

entries.topic: Does the UK get its oil and gas from Russia rather than Aberdeen?
entries.claim: Why is the Labour Government happy for Britain to get its oil and gas from Russia or Qatar, but not from Aberdeen?
entries.speaker: Claire Coutinho
entries.role: Shadow Energy Security and Net Zero Secretary
entries.discussionNote: With Keir Starmer at the G7 summit in France and Andy Burnham winning the Makerfield by-election overnight (declared in the early hours of 19 June 2026) to set up a likely Labour leadership challenge, the energy/North Sea row was the dominant Westminster set-piece of the 24-48 hour window. At a stand-in PMQs on 17 June, Deputy PM David Lammy faced shadow energy secretary Claire Coutinho, whose line — that Labour is 'happy for Britain to get its oil and gas from Russia or Qatar, but not from Aberdeen' — became the most-quoted clip of the exchange, carried by PA (via the Enfield Independent and others), El-Balad, the New Statesman and the Sunday Guardian PMQs reviews, and sharpened by Wes Streeting's reported leadership-pitch suggestion that Labour reverse its North Sea licensing ban. The Russia/Qatar versus Aberdeen framing was the freshest, cleanest checkable energy claim of the week.
entries.explanation: The headline framing is contradicted by the data on where the UK actually gets its gas, with one part (Qatar) partially true and the other (Russia) false. On Russia, the claim is plainly wrong: the UK has imported no gas from Russia since March 2022 and direct Russian LNG imports have been banned since 1 January 2023, while seaborne Russian crude oil was banned from December 2022 and oil products from February 2023 — so Britain does NOT get its oil and gas 'from Russia' at all. The Department for Energy Security and Net Zero's own Energy Trends figures show Russia at 0% of UK gas supply, with the bulk coming from Norway (about 47%) and domestic North Sea production (about 30%), then US LNG (about 15%); the largest crude-oil suppliers in 2024 were the US and Norway, not Russia. On Qatar, the claim is partially true but heavily overstated: Qatar is a real, if small, LNG supplier — roughly 1.2% of UK gas supply in 2025 (about 8% of LNG imports), down sharply from around 39% in 2011 — so it is far from a primary source. Crucially, the framing's central insinuation — that Labour favours foreign gas over Aberdeen — is misleading: the North Sea (Aberdeen's basin) plus Norway already provide the clear majority of UK gas, and the underlying dispute is about new exploration LICENCES in a naturally declining basin, not about whether existing North Sea oil and gas flows (Lammy's accurate retort that 'oil and gas is coming out of the North Sea 24/7'). There is a legitimate underlying point — that blocking new licences increases reliance on imported LNG over time, and the UK does import LNG including a sliver from Qatar — which keeps this above outright false, but invoking 'Russia', from which the UK buys nothing, makes the claim as used mostly false.
evidence: Claim (PMQs/DPMQs, 17 June 2026, Lammy deputising for Starmer at the G7): shadow energy secretary Claire Coutinho asked 'Why is the Labour Government happy for Britain to get its oil and gas from Russia or Qatar, but not from Aberdeen?'; Lammy replied that 'oil and gas is coming out of the North Sea 24/7' and that she 'left our country exposed to global fossil fuel markets'.

DESNZ Energy Trends (UK gas, 2026 release, 2025 data): Russia 0% of UK gas supply; Norway about 47% (≈322 TWh), domestic UK production about 30% (≈207 TWh), US LNG about 15% (≈104 TWh), Qatar about 1.2% (≈8 TWh) — so the UK gets none of its gas from Russia and only a small fraction from Qatar.

Russia bans: the UK has imported no gas from Russia since March 2022; direct Russian LNG imports prohibited from 1 January 2023; seaborne Russian crude oil banned from 5 December 2022 and oil products from 5 February 2023 (House of Commons Library; UK sanctions) — and in May 2026 the UK tightened sanctions further on maritime transport of Russian LNG.
Qatar trend: Qatari LNG has fallen from roughly 39% of UK gas in 2011 to about 1.8% in 2024 and ~1.2% in 2025, so it is a minor residual supplier, not a primary one.

Oil: the UK's largest crude-oil suppliers in 2024 were the US (~16 million tonnes, over a third) and Norway (~13 million tonnes, ~31%); no Russian crude is imported, so 'oil ... from Russia' is also false.

Legitimate underlying point: the row is about whether to issue NEW North Sea exploration licences in a declining basin; the North Sea plus Norway already supply the majority of UK gas, and reduced domestic output raises reliance on imported LNG (a route through which small Qatari volumes arrive) — but not on Russia.
entries.confidence: High — that the UK imports zero gas and oil from Russia (since 2022, with LNG and crude bans from 2022-23) is corroborated by DESNZ Energy Trends, NESO and the House of Commons Library briefing on fossil-fuel imports from Russia, and the Norway/domestic/US/Qatar shares come directly from DESNZ Energy Trends. The exact Coutinho quote and her shadow-energy-secretary role are confirmed by the PA wire report. The only soft spot is that the precise 2025 percentage decimals are provisional and the Qatar share varies slightly (1.2-1.8%) by year and dataset, but this does not change the verdict.

entries.topic: Will Labour's Plan for Change deliver 1.5 million homes?
entries.claim: Our Plan for Change will deliver 1.5 million new homes across the country.
entries.speaker: Keir Starmer
entries.role: Prime Minister; Leader of the Labour Party
entries.discussionNote: Housing was the dominant domestic battleground of the 24-48 hour window around the Makerfield by-election (polling day 18 June 2026), the contest widely framed as a referendum on Keir Starmer's leadership against would-be successor Andy Burnham, who campaigned hard on housing and council-house building. As No 10 defended the Prime Minister's record amid the leadership crisis, the government recycled its flagship 'Plan for Change' line that it 'will deliver 1.5 million new homes across the country' (pushed on the official Downing Street channels), while the Conservatives ran attack content ('they're miles off') and Bloomberg, Inside Housing, Big Issue and Homebuilding reported housebuilding 'unravelling' and 'on track to fall to its lowest level in more than a decade'. With Full Fact rating the pledge 'appears off track' and the MHCLG net-additions data showing a fall, the 1.5 million homes promise was the freshest, most-contested checkable number tied to the by-election.
entries.explanation: The 1.5 million homes figure is a genuine, official government pledge — but presenting it as something the Plan 'will deliver' is contradicted by both the latest hard data and the government's own independent forecaster, so as a delivery claim (rather than an aspiration) it is mostly false. The pledge is to build 1.5 million net additional homes in ENGLAND over this Parliament (Labour's manifesto, restated as a 'Plan for Change' milestone, with councils given a combined target of about 370,000 homes a year). On progress, Full Fact rates the pledge 'appears off track': there were about 342,100 net additional homes between 9 July 2024 and 15 March 2026 — roughly 22.8% of the 1.5 million target at close to the halfway point of the Parliament, when about 50% would be needed to be on a straight-line path. Crucially, the underlying trend is DOWN, not up: MHCLG's National Statistics show net additional dwellings in England FELL about 6% to 208,600 in 2024/25 (from 221,409 in 2023/24) — the third consecutive annual decline — against the roughly 300,000 a year needed to hit the target. Independent forecasters are blunter still: the OBR projects about 1.49 million net additions across the whole UK by 2029/30, which because housing is devolved translates to only around 1 million in England — roughly 500,000 short of the England-only pledge. Starmer himself has conceded the timetable is 'a little too ambitious'. The government's defence is that delivery will 'ramp up' late in the Parliament and that the OBR forecast does not yet price in the Planning and Infrastructure Bill, the National Housing Bank and the £39bn Affordable Homes Programme — so the target is not mathematically impossible. But on every current measure the trajectory points to a substantial miss, so stating the Plan 'will deliver' 1.5 million homes overstates a pledge that is behind schedule and forecast to fall well short.
evidence: Pledge / framing: Labour's manifesto promised '1.5 million new homes over the next parliament' (England only — housing minister Matthew Pennycook confirmed in October 2024 the target refers to England), restated as a 'Plan for Change' milestone with a council target of about 370,000 homes a year; No 10's official channels assert the Plan 'will deliver 1.5 million homes across the country'.

Full Fact government tracker: rates the pledge 'appears off track' — about 342,100 net additional homes between 9 July 2024 and 15 March 2026, roughly 22.8% of the 1.5 million target, with over five and a half more years needed at the current pace.

MHCLG (Housing supply: net additional dwellings, England, 2024/25, National Statistics, published 20 November 2025): net additions FELL about 6% to 208,600 (from 221,409 in 2023/24) — the third consecutive annual decline — comprising 190,600 new builds; the rate needed to hit the target is about 300,000 a year.

OBR: forecasts about 1.49 million net additions across the UK between 2024/25 and 2029/30, but as a UK-wide figure this implies only roughly 1 million in England — about 500,000 short of the England-only 1.5 million pledge.
Starmer has acknowledged the plan to deliver 1.5 million homes before the end of the Parliament is 'a little too ambitious', while insisting reforms will help meet it.

Government counter-context: ministers call the target 'stretching but achievable' and note the OBR forecast does not yet include the Planning and Infrastructure Bill, the National Housing Bank or the £39bn Affordable Homes Programme, which they argue will lift delivery in the later years of the Parliament.
entries.confidence: High — the pledge wording, the 'appears off track' rating and the 342,100 / 22.8% progress figures come from Full Fact's government tracker; the falling 208,600 net-additions figure for 2024/25 is MHCLG National Statistics; and the ~1.49m UK / ~1m England OBR forecast and the ~500,000 shortfall are corroborated across Full Fact, the OBR and multiple housing-sector outlets. The main judgement call is the verdict band: the 1.5 million figure is a real official target, so the claim is not fabricated, but as a delivery assertion it is contradicted by current data and independent forecasts, placing it in Mostly False.

entries.topic: Is UK inflation rising and the worst in the G7?
entries.claim: Inflation is up again and is the worst in the G7 — and you were left with 2% inflation.
entries.speaker: Kemi Badenoch
entries.role: Leader of the Opposition; Leader of the Conservative Party
entries.discussionNote: The dominant checkable line of the 24-48 hour window. At the final PMQs before summer recess on Wednesday 17 June 2026 — hours after the ONS published its May inflation release at 7am showing an unexpected rise — Kemi Badenoch repeatedly pressed Keir Starmer on the cost of living and possible autumn tax rises, hammering the charge that 'inflation is up again and is the worst in the G7' while the Conservatives had 'left him with 2% inflation'. Starmer's retort that she was 'talking the country down' became the headline clip. With the inflation print landing the same morning, Chancellor Rachel Reeves conceding 'there is more to do', and the exchange running across BBC live coverage, LBC and front pages amid the ongoing Labour leadership turmoil, the inflation/G7 attack line was the freshest, most-quoted checkable number of the day.
entries.explanation: Both halves of the headline attack are broadly supported, with caveats on precision. 'Up again' is accurate: CPI inflation had fallen to 2.8% in the 12 months to April 2026 (down from 3.3% in March, helped by the April Ofgem energy-price-cap cut), and the May 2026 figure released on the morning of PMQs rose back up — economists' consensus and the Bank of England's own April projection put it around 3.0-3.3% (Deutsche Bank forecast 3.01%, the BoE April MPR 3.26%), driven mainly by services inflation and airfares, with Reeves acknowledging 'there is more to do'. So inflation genuinely ticked up and remained well above the 2% target. 'The worst in the G7' is also well-founded as a comparison, and is the substantive checkable core: the OECD's September 2025 Economic Outlook forecast the UK to have the HIGHEST inflation in the G7 in both 2025 (3.5%) and 2026 (2.7%), ahead of Japan (3.1%), the US (2.7%), Germany (2.2%), Canada (2.0%), Italy (1.9%) and France (1.1%) for 2025, and the UK has been repeatedly flagged as the only G7 economy with headline inflation above 3%. The weakest element is the framing around 'left him with 2% inflation': CPI was 2.2% (not exactly 2%) in July 2024 when Labour took office — briefly at target — but that low point was substantially the product of falling energy base effects rather than a stable underlying position, and Full Fact notes inflation is 'still higher than it was when Labour came to power'. So the rhetorical handover figure is slightly rounded and strips out context, but the central claims — inflation rising again and the UK running the highest inflation in the G7 — are accurate.
evidence: ONS: CPI inflation was 2.8% in the 12 months to April 2026, down from 3.3% in March, with the April fall driven largely by the Ofgem energy price cap cut (electricity -8.4%, gas -4.4%); the May 2026 release was published at 7am on 17 June 2026, the morning of PMQs.

May 2026 consensus/forecasts pointed to a rise back to roughly 3.0-3.3%: Deutsche Bank forecast 3.01% (services inflation jumping to ~3.65%, airfares up >10% month-on-month), and the Bank of England's April Monetary Policy Report projected 3.26% headline CPI — confirming inflation 'up again' and well above the 2% target.

OECD Economic Outlook (23 September 2025): the UK is forecast to have the highest inflation in the G7 in both 2025 and 2026 — 3.5% in 2025 vs Japan 3.1%, US 2.7%, Germany 2.2%, Canada 2.0%, Italy 1.9% and France 1.1%; the UK has been repeatedly described as the only G7 economy with headline inflation above 3%.

ONS / Full Fact: CPI was 2.2% in July 2024 when Labour took office (close to, but not exactly, '2%'), peaked at 3.8% in summer 2025, and stood at 3% in January 2026 — so inflation is still higher than when Labour came to power, but the 2024 low owed much to energy base effects rather than a durable underlying position.

Chancellor Rachel Reeves responded to the May data that 'there is more to do' and that she is determined to 'deliver on our Plan for Change', implicitly accepting the rise; Starmer's PMQs reply was that Badenoch was 'talking the country down'.
entries.confidence: Medium-High — the 'worst in the G7' comparison is firmly corroborated by the OECD's September 2025 forecasts and consistent reporting that the UK is the only G7 economy above 3%, and the April 2.8% and July-2024 2.2% figures come directly from the ONS and Full Fact. The main soft spot is the exact May 2026 headline figure, released only hours before publication and not yet in the long-form ONS bulletin at time of writing; it is anchored here to the Bank of England and consensus forecasts (~3.0-3.3%) and same-day reporting of a rise, so the qualitative 'up again' claim is secure even though the precise decimal should be reconfirmed against the final ONS May 2026 bulletin.

entries.topic: Has the NHS hit its 18-week waiting-time target?
entries.claim: We've hit our NHS waiting-time target for the first time in years, with the biggest cut in the waiting list in a single month in 17 years — this government is cutting NHS waiting lists.
entries.speaker: Keir Starmer
entries.role: Prime Minister; Leader of the Labour Party
entries.discussionNote: With Andy Burnham vowing a leadership challenge if he wins the 18 June Makerfield by-election and around 95+ Labour MPs pressing Starmer to set out a departure timetable, the NHS has become the central plank of No 10's defence of the Prime Minister's record. As Burnham campaigned and Starmer insisted he 'will not walk away', a Downing Street spokesperson defended his leadership by pointing to the government 'cutting NHS waiting lists', recycling the NHS England announcement (published 14 May 2026) that the health service had 'hit its 18-week target' for the first time in years alongside a half-a-million drop in the list. Carried by NHS England, the BBC, the Health Foundation, the King's Fund and the Royal College of Surgeons, and slotted into the leadership-crisis coverage, the waiting-list boast is the freshest, cleanest checkable domestic number of the 24-48 hour window — and one independent health bodies had just heavily qualified.
entries.explanation: The specific numbers are accurate, but the word 'target' is doing the heavy lifting and the framing overstates how close the NHS is to its actual legal standard. On the figures, the claim checks out: NHS England data show that in March 2026, 65.3% of patients were waiting no more than 18 weeks from referral to treatment (RTT), so the government's INTERIM milestone of 65% by March 2026 was genuinely met — the first time an 18-week target of any kind had been hit in years. The waiting list also fell to about 7.11 million (its lowest in three and a half years), there were roughly 450,000 fewer waits over 18 weeks and around 515,000 fewer people on the list overall since July 2024, and the 312,000 year-on-year drop was the largest in 16 years, with Wes Streeting describing the latest monthly fall as the biggest single-month cut in 17 years. The crucial context is what 'target' means. The NHS constitutional STANDARD is that 92% of patients should wait under 18 weeks — and that standard has not been met since September 2015; at 65.3% the NHS is still about 27 percentage points short of it, with the full 92% pledge not due until March 2029. So the milestone hit was the government's own interim 65% staging post, not the constitutional standard most people associate with '18 weeks'. On top of that, the Health Foundation noted the NHS met this one headline waiting-time target but MISSED four of its other five key elective-care targets for March 2026, and the King's Fund warned a further roughly 1.9 million 18-week waits still have to be cleared to reach 92%, with Full Fact rating the overall pledge merely 'in progress' and a 'significant challenge'. The numbers are real and the direction of travel is genuinely down, but 'hit our target' invites people to hear 'the 18-week standard is met' when in fact the legal standard remains years away.
evidence: No 10 spokesperson (mid-June 2026, defending Starmer as Andy Burnham vowed a leadership challenge): the Labour government is 'cutting NHS waiting lists' — the central record-defence line, built on NHS England's 14 May 2026 announcement.

NHS England (published 14 May 2026): in March 2026, 65.3% of patients were waiting no longer than 18 weeks (RTT), meeting the government's interim target of 65% by March 2026; the waiting list fell to about 7.11 million (lowest in three and a half years), with around 450,000 fewer 18-week-plus waits and roughly 515,000 fewer on the list overall since July 2024.

NHS England / Wes Streeting: the 312,000 year-on-year fall was the largest in 16 years, and Streeting described the latest monthly drop as 'the biggest cut in waiting lists in a single month in 17 years'.

NHS constitutional standard: 92% of patients should wait under 18 weeks — last met in September 2015; at 65.3% the NHS remains about 27 percentage points short, and the government's own pledge to restore the 92% standard is not due until March 2029.
Health Foundation: the NHS met this headline waiting-time target but fell short on four of its other five key elective-care targets for March 2026, so progress is uneven.

King's Fund (Sarah Woolnough) / Royal College of Surgeons: roughly 1.9 million more 18-week-plus waits must still be cleared to reach the 92% standard under current trends, and 'there is still a long way to go'; Full Fact rates the 18-week pledge 'in progress' and a 'significant challenge', not achieved.
entries.confidence: High — the 65.3% March 2026 figure, the 7.11 million list total, the 450,000/515,000 reductions and the 312,000 (16-year) and single-month (17-year) falls all come directly from NHS England's 14 May 2026 release, and the central caveats (the 92% constitutional standard last met in September 2015, the ~27-point gap, the four missed targets and the ~1.9 million further reduction needed) are corroborated by the Health Foundation, the King's Fund, the Royal College of Surgeons and Full Fact's government tracker. The only soft spot is the exact wording attributed to Starmer/No 10, which paraphrases the government's recycled record-defence line rather than a single verbatim PMQs quote.

entries.topic: Has the government cut the asylum backlog by 46%?
entries.claim: This government has reduced immigration by 82%, cut the asylum backlog by 46%, and lifted half a million children out of poverty.
entries.speaker: Keir Starmer
entries.role: Prime Minister; Leader of the Labour Party
entries.discussionNote: Immigration and asylum have dominated the 24-48 hour window: the 'two-tier policing' row triggered by the murder of student Henry Nowak in Southampton ran into PMQs, with Nigel Farage clashing with Starmer and the Prime Minister denouncing Reform's response while defending his own record. Starmer's stock rebuttal list — that Labour has 'reduced immigration by 82%, cut the asylum backlog by 46%, and lifted half a million children out of poverty' — was the most-quoted defensive line of the exchange, recycled across PMQs, broadcast interviews and front-page coverage (GB News, LBC, IBTimes, Channel 4, CBS) as the row over asylum, hotels and policing kept immigration at the top of the news agenda. With the migration headline (82%) and defence already fact-checked earlier in the week, the asylum-backlog half of the claim is the freshest, cleanest checkable number — and one the Migration Observatory had just dissected.
entries.explanation: The 46% figure is genuinely accurate for the specific backlog Starmer is implicitly citing, but the word 'backlog' is doing a lot of selective work. On the narrow measure — the queue of people awaiting an INITIAL asylum decision — the claim checks out: the Migration Observatory and Home Office data show this backlog fell about 46% over the calendar year 2025, dropping from roughly 124,000-125,000 people (about 90,000 applications) at the end of 2024 to around 64,000 people (about 49,000 applications) at the end of 2025 — the lowest level in more than five years. A government spokesperson independently framed it as 'down nearly 50% under this government', consistent with the 46% figure. The problem is what the figure leaves out. First, the time period: the 46% is measured from the end of 2024 — already well into Labour's term — not from the system's peak, and the fall accelerated only after stalling through 2024. Second, and more importantly, cutting the initial-decision backlog by deciding cases faster has simply shifted the bottleneck downstream: faster processing produced about 80,000 initial refusals in 2025 (up from 46,000 in 2024), and the separate asylum APPEALS backlog roughly doubled to a record of around 70,000-80,000 cases — for the first time on record, the appeals queue now exceeds the initial-decision queue. The Migration Observatory's own headline on the data was that the government 'cuts one backlog, but increases another'. On top of that, roughly 31,000 asylum seekers were still in hotels at the end of 2025 — about 1,000 more than when Labour took office — so the overall pressure on the system has not eased the way 'cut the asylum backlog by 46%' implies. So: a real, correctly-stated number for the initial-decision backlog, but presented in a way that hides the offsetting surge in appeals and the still-elevated hotel population.
evidence: Starmer (PMQs, early-to-mid June 2026, defending his record during the 'two-tier policing' row): 'reduced immigration by 82%, cut the asylum backlog by 46%, and lifted half a million children out of poverty' — the government's standard rebuttal line, repeated across interviews and the Commons.

Migration Observatory (analysis of end-2025 Home Office data, released 26 Feb 2026): the backlog of people awaiting an INITIAL asylum decision fell about 46%, from just under 125,000 people at the end of 2024 to roughly 64,000 (about 49,000 applications) at the end of 2025 — the smallest since 2020.

Migration Observatory headline framing: faster processing 'cuts one backlog, but increases another' — Ministry of Justice data show the number of people awaiting an asylum APPEAL roughly doubled to around 70,000 (Sept 2025), and other figures put the appeals backlog at a record ~80,000 by end-2025, exceeding the initial-decision backlog for the first time on record.

Home Office / PA (March 2026): around 108,000 initial decisions were made in 2025 (63% more than 2024), but this produced about 80,000 initial refusals (up from 46,000 in 2024) and around 63,000 new appeals lodged against only ~26,000 decided — driving the appeals surge.

Hotels: roughly 31,000 asylum seekers were housed in hotels at 31 December 2025 — about 5,000 fewer than September 2025, but still around 1,000 more than when Labour took office in mid-2024, so overall accommodation pressure remained high.

Time-period caveat: the 46% is measured from end-2024, already inside Labour's term, not from the all-time peak of about 132,000 applications (161,000 people) reached in 2019-2022, so it understates how far the queue had already moved before this government's processing push.
entries.confidence: High — the 46% fall in the initial-decision backlog is confirmed by both the Migration Observatory and a government spokesperson citing Home Office data, and the offsetting roughly-doubled appeals backlog, the ~31,000 hotel figure and the 2025 decisions/refusals numbers are all on the record from the Migration Observatory, the Ministry of Justice and Home Office statistics via PA. The only soft spots are minor reporting differences in the appeals total (~70,000 to ~80,000 depending on the cut-off date) and that 'backlog' is undefined in the claim itself.

Migration Observatory — Asylum hotel numbers remain high as processing cuts one backlog but increases another

entries.topic: Is this the biggest defence spending increase since the Cold War?
entries.claim: This government will begin the biggest sustained increase in defence spending since the end of the Cold War.
entries.speaker: Keir Starmer
entries.role: Prime Minister; Leader of the Labour Party
entries.discussionNote: The single biggest political story of the 24-48 hour window was the defence-funding crisis engulfing the government: Defence Secretary John Healey resigned on 11 June 2026, followed hours later by armed forces minister Al Carns, accusing the Treasury of refusing to commit the resources needed to defend the country and warning the Defence Investment Plan would take spending only to 2.68% of GDP by 2030 when 3% is needed. As Starmer reshuffled (appointing Dan Jarvis on 12 June) and defended his record in a BBC interview, his stock line — that Labour is delivering 'the biggest sustained increase in defence spending since the end of the Cold War' — became the most-scrutinised claim of the week, carried by the BBC, Bloomberg, CNN, the Globe and Mail, Forces News and the New Statesman, and directly contradicted by his own departing minister. The £13.4bn headline figure underpinning it was branded 'misleadingly large' and 'silly games with numbers' by the IFS, putting the claim at the centre of the resignation row.
entries.explanation: The 'since the Cold War' framing is defensible on one measure and misleading on another, and the headline number attached to it is contested. Measured in cash and real terms, the planned rise is genuinely large: after decades of decline, the government is lifting NATO-qualifying defence spending from about 2.3% of GDP in 2024 to a committed 2.6% by April 2027, with a stated ambition of 3% in the next Parliament — and the IFS broadly accepts that, as a sustained real-terms uplift, this is the biggest since the Cold War ended, because spending had been on a long downward path since the late 1980s. The misleading part is the implied scale. As a SHARE OF GDP, even 2.6% or 3% remains far below actual Cold War levels, when UK defence spending ran at roughly 4-6% of GDP through the 1980s — so 'since the end of the Cold War' quietly excludes the Cold War's own much higher spending. More damagingly, the £13.4bn-a-year figure the government uses to dramatise the increase was branded 'misleadingly large' by the IFS: it compares cash spending in 2027/28 with 2024/25 rather than with what spending would otherwise have been, and the genuine real-terms uplift from the 0.2-percentage-point rise is closer to £6bn — a figure Healey himself conceded. Above all, the boast was made in the same week Starmer's own Defence Secretary resigned saying the plan 'falls well short of what is required' and reaches only 2.68% of GDP by 2030, so the 'biggest increase' line is real in direction but oversells both its size and its adequacy.
evidence: Starmer (BBC interview, 11-12 June 2026, defending his record after John Healey's resignation): 'this government will begin the biggest sustained increase in defence spending since the end of the Cold War' — the government's standard framing first used when the 2.5%-by-2027 plan was announced in February 2025.

Full Fact / NATO: UK defence spending was about 2.3% of GDP in 2024 and 2.4% in 2025; the government has committed to 2.6% (NATO definition, including pensions and the Integrated Security Fund) by April 2027, with an ambition of 3% in the next Parliament — a genuine real-terms increase after a long post-Cold-War decline.

House of Commons Library / IFS: UK defence spending ran at roughly 4-6% of GDP through the 1980s and has fallen sharply since; today's ~2.3-2.6% remains well below actual Cold War levels, so 'biggest increase SINCE the Cold War' excludes the far higher Cold-War-era share of GDP.

IFS (Paul Johnson and Ben Zaranko): the £13.4bn headline figure is 'misleadingly large' — it compares 2027/28 cash with 2024/25 rather than the counterfactual; an extra 0.2% of GDP is about £6bn (the size of the aid-budget cut), and Johnson accused the PM of 'silly games with numbers' with 'totally inconsistent figures'.

John Healey, then Defence Secretary, conceded the real-terms increase 'would be something over £6 billion' and that 'the definition of defence numbers can be done in different ways'; he then resigned on 11 June 2026 saying the plan 'falls well short of what is required' and reaches only 2.68% of GDP by 2030 against a needed 3%.

Context: armed forces minister Al Carns resigned hours after Healey, and Dan Jarvis was appointed Defence Secretary on 12 June 2026; the Defence Investment Plan is due before the NATO summit on 7 July 2026, so the underlying plan was still unpublished when the claim was repeated.
entries.confidence: High — the historical GDP-share comparison (4-6% in the 1980s versus ~2.3-2.6% now) comes from the House of Commons Library and IFS, the current and committed percentages are confirmed by Full Fact and NATO data, and the critique of the £13.4bn figure is on the record from the IFS (Paul Johnson and Ben Zaranko) and was effectively conceded by Healey himself. The only soft spot is that 'sustained increase' is partly a forward-looking projection dependent on a Defence Investment Plan not yet published.

entries.topic: Who cut net migration — Labour or the Conservatives
entries.claim: The government has cut net migration by 80%. I'm not sure the Conservatives should be taking credit for this — it's happened on this government's watch.
entries.speaker: Andy Burnham
entries.role: Mayor of Greater Manchester; Labour candidate in the Makerfield by-election (18 June 2026)
entries.discussionNote: With the Makerfield by-election on 18 June shaping up as the most-watched contest of the month — Andy Burnham, a potential Starmer leadership challenger, against Reform's Robert Kenyon — the BBC by-election debate put immigration front and centre. Burnham's clash with Conservative candidate Michael Winstanley over who deserves credit for the falling numbers was the debate's most-quoted exchange, written up by AOL, MSN and the local press, and fact-checked head-on by Full Fact on 10-12 June ('Are the Conservatives or Labour responsible for the fall in net migration?'). It sits on top of immigration's continued status as the public's number-one issue and a week of Makerfield-related disinformation (AI 'Burnham' images, miscaptioned riot clips), making the credit-claiming over the migration fall one of the most-circulated factual disputes of the 24-48 hour window.
entries.explanation: The headline number is roughly right but the credit-claiming is the misleading part. On the raw figure, Burnham is close: ONS provisional data show net migration fell from a peak of 944,000 in the year ending March 2023 to 171,000 in the year ending December 2025 — an 82% fall, so '80%' is a fair rounding. The problem is the framing 'I'm not sure the Conservatives should be taking credit... it's happened on this government's watch', which implies Labour drove the reduction. The evidence points the other way. Measured from the year ending June 2024 — the last quarter before Labour took office in July 2024 — net migration fell about 74%, not 80%, so most of the headline drop predates this government. More importantly, both the ONS and the Migration Observatory attribute the fall mainly to visa rule changes the previous Conservative government introduced in early 2024 (restrictions on dependants of international students and care workers, and tighter Skilled Worker rules): the ONS says the drop 'may be partly because of immigration rule changes, which were introduced in 2024 and 2025', and the Migration Observatory says it was 'largely driven by policies first introduced by the previous government', with Labour's own May 2025 measures 'not yet fully visible' in the 2025 figures. So the 82% fall is real and did continue under Labour, but the bulk of it stems from Conservative-era policy still working through the data — making Burnham's bid to deny the Conservatives credit the weakest part of the claim. (His Conservative opponent Michael Winstanley's mirror-image claim, that the fall was due to 'policies brought in under the tail end of the last Conservative government', is closer to the expert consensus, though Full Fact notes no one can yet attribute the fall precisely.)
evidence: ONS Long-term international migration, provisional (released 21 May 2026): net migration 171,000 for the year ending December 2025, down from a revised peak of 944,000 in the year ending March 2023 — a fall of about 82%, consistent with Burnham's rounded '80%'.

Full Fact (10-12 June 2026): net migration fell about 82% from the YE March 2023 peak to YE December 2025, but only about 74% since the year ending June 2024, just before Labour took office in July 2024 — so most of the fall predates this government.

ONS: 'The drop in total immigration in the latest year may be partly because of immigration rule changes, which were introduced in 2024 and 2025'; the single biggest driver was a 47% fall in non-EU+ work-related arrivals in 2025.

Migration Observatory: the decline 'results from the measures of both the current and the previous administrations' and was 'largely driven by policies first introduced by the previous [Conservative] government' — chiefly the early-2024 restrictions on student and care-worker dependants; Labour's May 2025 changes were 'not yet fully visible' in the 2025 statistics.

Conservative candidate Michael Winstanley argued in the same debate that the fall was 'due to the policies that were brought in under the tail end of the last Conservative government where we put a restriction on visas' — a framing closer to the ONS/Migration Observatory assessment.

Caveat on attribution: Full Fact concludes 'we don't know for sure exactly which factors have driven the fall in net migration', and the 171,000 figure is provisional and prone to upward revision, so neither side can claim precise credit.
entries.confidence: High — the 82% headline fall and the 171,000 / 944,000 figures come directly from ONS provisional migration statistics, and the attribution caveats (74% under Labour; the fall largely driven by Conservative-era 2024 rule changes) are corroborated by Full Fact, the ONS commentary and the Migration Observatory. The only soft spot is that exact policy attribution is inherently uncertain and the latest figure is provisional.

entries.topic: Welfare spending under the Conservatives
entries.claim: On their watch, defence spending went down and welfare spending went up — £88 billion overall, with £33 billion alone under the shadow Chancellor, Sir Mel Stride.
entries.speaker: Keir Starmer
entries.role: Prime Minister; Leader of the Labour Party
entries.discussionNote: With anti-immigration riots in Belfast and the delayed Defence Investment Plan dominating the week, Starmer used PMQs on 10 June 2026 to turn Badenoch's attack on funding back onto the Conservative record, deploying the '£88 billion' welfare-rise line as his central rebuttal. The figure was clipped for Labour's official social channels ('Under the Tories, the welfare bill went up by £88 billion'), amplified by LBC, ITV News and the Spectator and New Statesman PMQs write-ups, and slotted into the running welfare-versus-defence-versus-tax argument that has run through Westminster all month — making it one of the most-circulated factual claims of the 24–48 hour window, and the headline number the government wants to define the Conservatives by.
entries.explanation: The headline number is broadly right but the framing is doing a lot of work. Total real-terms welfare/social-security spending in Great Britain did rise substantially across the Conservatives' 2010–2024 period: Resolution Foundation analysis (its 'Ratchets, retrenchment and reform' study and follow-ups) shows welfare rising as a share of the economy from about 10% of GDP in 2007–08 to 11.2% in 2024–25, with a real-terms increase of roughly £44 billion in just the last five years of that period (2019–20 to 2024–25 prices), so an £88bn cumulative real-terms rise over the full Conservative span is a defensible order of magnitude and matches the figure Labour has published. The crucial context Starmer omits is composition: the overwhelming majority of the increase was driven by the state pension under the triple lock (about £21bn of the recent rise) and by health and disability benefits (about £24bn of the recent rise) — categories largely outside day-to-day government control or actively protected by all parties — while spending on mainstream working-age, non-health benefits actually FELL in real terms over the same years, with benefit-receiving working-age households losing on average about £1,500 a year from cuts such as the benefit cap, two-child limit and the 2010s freezes. So 'welfare spending went up £88 billion on their watch' is numerically fair but rhetorically implies Tory largesse on out-of-work benefits, when the rise was concentrated in pensions and disability/incapacity support that the Conservatives were cutting elsewhere. The '£33 billion under Sir Mel Stride' sub-claim attributes a slice of that rise to Stride's tenure spanning DWP and the Treasury; it is plausible but harder to pin to a single published figure than the £88bn total.
evidence: Hansard, PMQs 10 June 2026: Starmer said that on the Conservatives' watch 'defence spending went down and welfare spending went up — £88 billion overall, with £33 billion alone under the shadow Chancellor, Sir Mel Stride', arguing they neither reformed welfare nor invested in the armed forces.

Resolution Foundation: total welfare spending rose as a share of GDP from about 10% in 2007–08 to 11.2% in 2024–25, and increased about £44bn in real terms between 2019–20 (£280bn) and 2025–26 (£324bn) alone — consistent with an ~£88bn cumulative real-terms rise across the full 2010–2024 Conservative period.

Of the recent £44bn rise, about £21bn was pensioner benefits (driven by the triple lock and an ageing population) and about £24bn was non-pensioner disability and incapacity benefits; spending on other working-age benefits fell by roughly £1bn in real terms.

Resolution Foundation: between 2010–11 and 2024–25, working-age households receiving benefits lost on average about £1,500 a year in real terms from policy changes (benefit cap, two-child limit, multi-year freezes) — i.e. mainstream working-age welfare was cut, not expanded, over the period.

Labour repeated the figure on its official channels ('Under the Tories, the welfare bill went up by £88 billion'), confirming the wording was a deliberate, central government line rather than an off-the-cuff number.

Caveat on attribution: the '£33bn under Mel Stride' element folds together periods in different roles and is not anchored to a single published primary figure, so it is less verifiable than the £88bn total.
entries.confidence: Medium-High — the direction and rough magnitude of the £88bn real-terms rise are well supported by Resolution Foundation analysis of DWP/OBR welfare data, and the composition caveats (pensions and disability driving the rise while working-age benefits were cut) are firmly documented. The precise £88bn cumulative figure rests on a specific real-terms baseline and deflator that the government has not fully published, and the '£33bn under Stride' sub-figure could not be confirmed against a single primary source, so the exact numbers carry some uncertainty even though the overall picture is sound.

entries.topic: Defence Investment Plan funding gap
entries.claim: Military chiefs have said the Defence Investment Plan would need £28 billion to be fully funded — will that requirement be met in full?
entries.speaker: Kemi Badenoch
entries.role: Leader of the Opposition; Leader of the Conservative Party
entries.discussionNote: Defence dominated PMQs on 10 June 2026, with Badenoch devoting all six of her questions to the delayed Defence Investment Plan (DIP) and pressing Starmer on a reported £28bn funding gap and whether he would rule out tax rises to close it. Starmer declined to confirm the figure or rule out tax rises, saying only the plan would come 'before the NATO summit'. The clash — framed around Badenoch's charge of 'total paralysis' and 'dithering' — led Spectator, GB News, Yorkshire Post and other coverage, and sat on top of a week of warnings from Chief of the Defence Staff Sir Richard Knighton that Britain is 'running out of time' to rearm, making the £28bn figure the most-scrutinised number in UK politics this week.
entries.explanation: Badenoch is accurately repeating a figure that has been widely reported, but she presents a briefed, unpublished number as a settled official requirement, which it is not. Multiple outlets (Defense News, US News, Reuters via Daily Maverick, and the GB News/Spectator and Yorkshire Post PMQs coverage) report that 'military chiefs have warned Starmer there is a £28 billion shortfall in funding over the next four years', and that this gap is the reason the DIP has been repeatedly delayed since last year. So the £28bn figure is genuine in the sense that it is the number circulating in credible reporting and attributed to defence chiefs. Two important caveats. First, it is a media-reported figure attributed to unnamed 'military chiefs', not a costing published by the MoD, NAO or OBR — the DIP itself was still unpublished when Badenoch spoke, so 'fully funded' is being measured against a plan the public cannot yet see. Second, the framing implies a fixed, agreed requirement, whereas it represents the gap between what chiefs say is needed for 'warfighting readiness' and the funding currently pencilled in, within an envelope the government says rises to 2.6% of GDP by April 2027 (from 2.4% in 2025) and a stated ambition of 3% in the next Parliament. The number is therefore accurately quoted but rests on briefing rather than a primary published source, and its status as a hard 'requirement' is contested.
evidence: Defense News (5 June 2026): 'Media reports say military chiefs have warned Starmer that there is a £28 billion ($38 billion) shortfall in funding over the next four years', cited as the reason for the DIP delay.

The figure is corroborated across US News, Daily Maverick (Reuters), Global Banking & Finance and the GB News/Spectator and Yorkshire Post PMQs write-ups — but none attributes it to a named official or a published MoD/NAO/OBR document; it is consistently described as a media-reported, briefed figure.

Spectator PMQs coverage (10 June 2026): Badenoch 'pointed out that military chiefs had said the DIP would need £28 billion to be fully funded' and asked 'will that requirement be met in full?'; Starmer replied ministers were 'working through the details'.

The DIP, intended to fund the move to 'warfighting readiness', was originally due last autumn and remained unpublished; Defence Secretary John Healey said it would be published before the NATO summit on 7 July 2026.

Context on the funding envelope: Full Fact and the House of Commons Library record UK defence spending at about 2.4% of GDP in 2025, rising to a committed 2.6% by April 2027 (Starmer: '£270 billion over the Parliament'), with a stated ambition of 3% in the next Parliament and 3.5% by 2035 under the new NATO target.

Chief of the Defence Staff Sir Richard Knighton warned on 5 June 2026 that the UK faces its most dangerous security environment since the Cold War and is 'running out of time' to modernise, lending weight to the chiefs' funding warnings.
entries.confidence: Medium — the £28bn figure is consistently reported across several independent outlets and attributed to military chiefs, and Badenoch quotes it accurately, but it originates in briefing rather than a published primary costing (MoD/NAO/OBR), so the precise figure and its status as a fixed 'requirement' cannot be fully verified against a primary source. The surrounding GDP figures are confirmed by Full Fact and the Commons Library.

entries.topic: Teacher recruitment target
entries.claim: Latest stats show we're over 70% of the way to our target to recruit 6,500 teachers in the places they're needed most — 4,654 more teachers in those schools and colleges compared to 2024.
entries.speaker: Bridget Phillipson
entries.role: Secretary of State for Education
entries.discussionNote: The DfE's annual school workforce statistics, published 4 June 2026, showed the total teaching workforce shrinking for the first time since 2019 even as ministers claimed progress on the flagship '6,500 new teachers' pledge. Phillipson's '70% of the way' framing was picked up by Schools Week, TES, Channel 4 FactCheck, the Coventry Observer and Guido Fawkes (who accused her of 'spinning' the pledge), fact-checked by Full Fact, and queried by the Commons Education Committee — making it one of the most-scrutinised domestic-policy claims of the week and the central education flashpoint as overall teacher numbers fell.
entries.explanation: The specific number Phillipson cites is accurate, but it is true only because of how narrowly the target is defined — and it sits alongside an overall fall in teacher numbers that the headline framing obscures. Against the manifesto pledge of at least 6,500 more teachers in secondary schools, special schools/PRUs and further-education colleges by the end of the Parliament (baseline 2023/24), the DfE's 4,654 figure is genuine and is indeed about 72% of the way there. But three caveats matter. First, the target deliberately excludes primary and nursery teachers, and the total full-time-equivalent teaching workforce in state-funded schools actually fell by about 1,900 (from 468,272 to 466,372) between November 2024 and November 2025 — the first contraction of the overall workforce since 2019. Second, it is a net-change measure, not 6,500 newly hired individuals, and most of the progress came in the first year (Nov 2023-Nov 2024); only roughly 670 were added in the latest year. Third, the 4,654 total leans heavily on further-education colleges (1,646 of it), whose inclusion critics say was added to the pledge after the fact; the DfE's own release credits just 3,008 secondary and special-school teachers. So the claim is literally correct on its own terms, which is why Full Fact rates the pledge 'appears on track', but the '70% of the way' line gives a misleadingly rosy impression of teacher supply overall.
evidence: DfE School Workforce in England statistics (published 4 June 2026): 4,654 additional teachers recruited towards the 6,500 target since the 2023/24 baseline — about 72% of the way there.
DfE breakdown: that 4,654 comprises 3,008 secondary and special-school teachers plus 1,646 in further education; the FE element is the contested part of the count.
Total FTE teachers in state-funded schools fell by about 1,900 (468,272 to 466,372) between November 2024 and November 2025 — the first fall in the overall school workforce since 2019.

Full Fact (June 2026): the 4,654 figure and '70%-plus' framing are accurate, but the target excludes primary and nursery teachers; most progress (around 4,000 of the gain) came in the first year, with only roughly 670 added in the latest year.
The pledge is a net-change target ('at least 6,500 more teachers... at the end of this Parliament than at the start'), not 6,500 brand-new hires, and Full Fact rates it 'appears on track'.
Primary teachers fell about 1.3% and secondary teachers about 0.4% year-on-year; special/PRU teachers rose about 3.9% (DfE, 4 June 2026).
entries.confidence: High — the figures come directly from the DfE's 4 June 2026 School Workforce statistics and are corroborated independently by Full Fact and Schools Week, which reach the same conclusion: the number is right but the framing omits the falling overall workforce and the narrow target definition.

entries.topic: Welfare spending versus income tax
entries.claim: We're now in a situation for the first time ever where the amount that we are paying in benefits is more than the government is taking in income tax.
entries.speaker: Katie Lam
entries.role: Conservative MP for Weald of Kent and Opposition Assistant Whip
entries.discussionNote: The line that welfare now exceeds income tax 'for the first time ever' has become one of the most-repeated Conservative and Reform attack lines of the welfare debate, deployed by Katie Lam on BBC Radio 4's World at One and echoed almost word-for-word by Robert Jenrick, Helen Whately, Kemi Badenoch and Luke Evans across broadcast and social media. Full Fact has had to fact-check the framing more than once, and it remains a high-reach talking point as the parties spar over the size of the benefits bill.
entries.explanation: Lam's underlying fact — that total welfare spending currently exceeds income tax receipts — is correct, but the 'for the first time ever' framing is the opposite of the truth. Using OBR figures, Full Fact and the IFS find welfare (which includes the state pension, not just out-of-work benefits) has been higher than income tax receipts every year for at least the last 13 years, going back to at least 2013/14. Far from being the first time, 2025/26 is expected to be the LAST year for the foreseeable future that welfare exceeds income tax: the OBR forecasts the gap narrowing sharply and the positions reversing in 2026/27, with income tax staying ahead through to the end of the forecast in 2030/31. The figures also do not cleanly map onto 'workers paying for non-workers' — many workers receive benefits and many non-workers, including pensioners, pay income tax.
evidence: Full Fact (June 2026), citing OBR figures: welfare spending has been higher than income tax receipts every year for at least the last 13 years, going back to at least 2013/14 — not for the first time.

IFS (Tom Waters): welfare spending is forecast to be lower than income tax receipts in 2026/27 'for the first time since at least 2013/14', and to remain that way until the end of the forecast period in 2030/31.
The gap between welfare and income tax narrowed significantly in 2025/26, so 2025/26 is expected to be the last year — not the first — that welfare exceeds income tax.

Welfare here includes the state pension alongside universal credit and other benefits, so the totals are not a clean measure of money paid by workers versus money spent on non-workers; many workers claim benefits and many pensioners pay income tax.
The same 'first time ever' framing was used by other senior figures including Robert Jenrick, Helen Whately, Kemi Badenoch and Luke Evans, and was rated false in each case.
entries.confidence: High — corroborated by two Full Fact analyses and the IFS, all drawing on OBR public-finances data; the OBR is the underlying primary source.

entries.topic: Small boat Channel crossings
entries.claim: We'd stop the boats — we'd stop 1,000 people coming in a day.
entries.speaker: Robert Kenyon
entries.role: Reform UK candidate, Makerfield by-election (18 June 2026)
entries.discussionNote: Immigration remains the single most-cited issue facing Britain, and this claim landed on BBC Question Time (4 June) in the run-up to the high-profile Makerfield by-election on 18 June, where Reform UK's Robert Kenyon faces Labour's Andy Burnham. The contest is one of the most-watched political events of the month. Kenyon's '1,000 people a day' line was clipped and shared by LBC, fact-checked by Full Fact, and drew sustained social-media reaction, keeping it near the top of the week's immigration debate.
entries.explanation: Kenyon's framing — that stopping the boats would stop '1,000 people coming in a day' — presents 1,000 daily arrivals as the normal current rate. That overstates the real figure by roughly ten times. Home Office data show that since Labour took office on 5 July 2024, small-boat arrivals have averaged about 106 people per day overall (around 274 per day if days with zero crossings are excluded). Days of 1,000 or more arrivals are rare: there have been just 12 such days in the entire dataset since 2018, and only four since Labour took office. The Migration Observatory puts total crossings at roughly 41,000 in both 2024 and 2025 (about 112 a day), with 2026 running sharply lower — around 9,000 in the first five months. There is a grain of truth that 1,000-arrival days do occasionally happen in peak summer conditions, but presenting that as the everyday rate is a significant exaggeration.
evidence: Full Fact (June 2026): small-boat arrivals have averaged about 106 per day since Labour took office on 5 July 2024 (274 per day excluding days with no crossings) — not 1,000.
Full Fact: only 12 days since the dataset began on 1 January 2018 have seen 1,000+ small-boat arrivals; just four of those fall since July 2024 (31 May 2025; 6 and 19 September 2025; 8 October 2025).
Migration Observatory: roughly 41,000 crossings in 2024 and about 41,000 in 2025 — an average of around 112 people per day across the year.
Migration Observatory: about 9,000 people crossed in the first five months of 2026, around 38% lower than the same period in 2025, so the current daily rate is well below even the long-run average.
The highest annual total to date was about 46,000 in 2022; no year has come close to the ~365,000 that a sustained rate of 1,000 a day would imply.
entries.confidence: High — corroborated by Full Fact's analysis of Home Office small-boat arrivals data and the Migration Observatory's Channel crossings briefing, two independent sources.

entries.topic: UK economic growth vs the G7
entries.claim: Britain is the fastest growing economy in the G7 — that is proof our economic plan is working.
entries.speaker: Rachel Reeves
entries.role: Chancellor of the Exchequer
entries.discussionNote: With Keir Starmer fighting for his job amid a Labour leadership crisis, Reeves leaned hard on the Q1 2026 GDP figures (released 14 May) as the government's central defence, repeating the 'fastest growing economy in the G7' line in interviews and party briefings through late May and into early June. The boast was contested by Conservative and Reform commentators and picked apart by independent statisticians, making it one of the most-argued economic claims of the week and a recurring flashpoint in coverage of Labour's record.
entries.explanation: Taken narrowly, the claim is accurate: ONS data show UK real GDP grew 0.6% in Q1 2026 (January to March), the strongest of any G7 economy that quarter — ahead of the United States (0.5%), Canada (0.4%), Germany (0.3%), Italy (0.2%) and France (0.0%). Real GDP per head also rose 0.6%, the fastest in four years. But the unqualified phrasing implies sustained leadership, and that does not hold. In Q4 2025 the UK was in the lower half of the G7 (0.2% growth), and across the whole of 2025 its 1.4% annual growth trailed the US (2.1%) and Canada (1.7%). The IMF's January 2026 forecast puts the UK third in the G7 for 2026, behind the US and Canada. So the UK led the G7 for a single quarter, not on a sustained basis — and Labour's own manifesto pledge was for 'the highest sustained growth in the G7', which Full Fact rates as 'appears off track'.
evidence: ONS GDP first quarterly estimate (released 14 May 2026), Table 5: UK Q1 2026 quarterly growth 0.6% — ahead of the US (0.5%), Canada (0.4%), Germany (0.3%), Italy (0.2%) and France (0.0%).
ONS: UK real GDP per head rose 0.6% in Q1 2026 (0.9% year-on-year), the fastest quarterly per-head growth in about four years; Q4 2025 growth was revised to 0.2%.
ONS Table 5: for full-year 2025 the UK grew 1.4%, behind the US (2.1%) and Canada (1.7%) though ahead of the European G7 members.
IMF World Economic Outlook update (January 2026): the UK is forecast to be the third fastest-growing G7 economy in 2026 (1.3%) and 2027 (1.5%), behind the US and Canada.

Full Fact government tracker: Labour's pledge of 'the highest sustained growth in the G7' is rated 'appears off track' — the UK has led on GDP per head in only one quarter (Q1 2025) since July 2024, not consistently.
entries.confidence: High — the single-quarter ranking is confirmed by the ONS first quarterly estimate (with its own G7 comparison table) and corroborated by OECD Q1 2026 data, while the sustained-growth caveat rests on IMF projections and Full Fact's tracker.
